scenario: A team has DMN models stored as files. They wonder if a DRD (Decision Requirements Diagram) is a separate file or part of the DMN file.
question: DRD vs DMN file structure?

options.text: A DRD is a visualization / structural representation of decision dependencies within a DMN file -- it's not a separate file. dmn) contains the decisions, their inputs / outputs, and dependency relationships (information requirements, knowledge requirements). The DRD is the visual graph of these dependencies; rendered by tools (Modeler) from the file's structure. Modular alternatives: split decisions across multiple DMN files with imports if very complex; depends on Camunda 8 support for DMN imports.

A DRD is stored as a separate file from the decision table files, similar to how BPMN process files are kept separate from form files. This misrepresents DMN file organisation. dmn file contains all decisions, their input and output columns, dependency relationships expressed as informationRequirement elements, and the DMNDI section with visual layout information. The DRD is the visual representation derived from this single file, not stored in a separate file.

Whether a DRD exists as a separate diagram file or as part of the decision file depends entirely on which modelling tool is used, with different vendors making different choices. dmn XML structure. Camunda Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler both follow this standard, keeping DRD visual layout in the same file as the decision logic.

DMN models contain only decision tables and expression logic; there is no diagram or visual representation concept in the DMN standard. This is factually wrong. 3 defines the Decision Requirements Graph (DRG) as a formal model and the Decision Requirements Diagram (DRD) as its visual representation. The dmndi:DMNDI section in a DMN XML file stores visual positioning data for rendering the DRD canvas. Camunda Web Modeler displays the DRD as the default view when opening a DMN file.

a.text: Correct. DMN file vs DRD relationship: DMN file (.dmn): XML format following the OMG DMN standard. Contents: root element. One or more elements (each with logic -- typically decision tables or boxed expressions). elements (external inputs to the DRD). for BKM (per Q24). for documentation references. , -- relationships between elements. Diagram info (DI): section with visual positioning info. DRD (Decision Requirements Diagram): Visual representation of the decision dependencies declared in the DMN file. Generated by tools (Web Modeler, Desktop Modeler) from the file's structure. Shows: Decision nodes (rectangles). InputData (ovals). BKM (with hat icon). Knowledge sources (documents). Arrows indicating dependencies. Single file holds everything: All decisions in one DMN file. DRD generated from that file. One file, one DRD (in standard DMN). Multiple decisions in one file: Typical: a DRD might have 5-10 decisions composing a complex one. One file holds the whole composition. Modular alternatives: Multiple DMN files with imports: standard DMN supports to reference definitions in other DMN files. Camunda 8 support for DMN imports: verify per version. Trade-off: modularity vs deployment complexity. Typical workflow: 1. Modeler opens a DMN file. 2. Sees the DRD canvas. 3. Edits decisions / dependencies. 4. Saves the .dmn file. 5. Deploys via Web Modeler or zbctl.
